introduction.p: Chapter began with the same idea that built single-variable integration: cut, multiply, add, and pass to a limit. The interval became a region. The tiny length dx became a tiny area dA, or, when orientation matters, dx\wedge dy.
The hardest part of most double integral problems is not the integration. It is deciding what the small pieces look like.

subsection.title: The chapter in one decision path
subsection.p: A double integral begins with a question: \text{What is being added, and over what region?}

If the region is a rectangle, iterated integrals usually go straight through. If the region has curved edges, draw it first. If the region is circular, polar coordinates may turn the picture into simple bounds. If the region is infinite or the function blows up, use an improper limit.

figure.caption: Most double integral problems are solved by matching the region to the right description.

subsection.p:
Here are the main formulas from the chapter.
\begin{array}{c|c} \text{Quantity} \amp \text{Double integral} \\ \hline \text{area of }R \amp \displaystyle \operatorname{Area}(R)=\iint_R 1\,dA \\[3mm] \text{volume under }z=f(x,y) \amp \displaystyle V=\iint_R f(x,y)\,dA \\[3mm] \text{average value} \amp \displaystyle f_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{1}{\operatorname{Area}(R)}\iint_R f\,dA \\[4mm] \text{mass of a lamina} \amp \displaystyle M=\iint_R \rho\,dA \\[3mm] \text{moments} \amp \displaystyle M_y=\iint_R x\rho\,dA, \qquad M_x=\iint_R y\rho\,dA \\[4mm] \text{center of mass} \amp \displaystyle \bar x=\frac{M_y}{M}, \qquad \bar y=\frac{M_x}{M} \\[4mm] \text{moment of inertia about the origin} \amp \displaystyle I_O=\iint_R (x^2+y^2)\rho\,dA \\[4mm] \text{probability over }R \amp \displaystyle P((X,Y)\in R)=\iint_R p(x,y)\,dA \end{array}
For polar coordinates, \boxed{x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta,\qquad dA=r\,dr\,d\theta.} The extra factor r is the area-scaling factor for a small polar rectangle.

subsection.title: Worked review example: drawing the region before integrating
subsection.p: Let R be the region bounded by y=x^2\qquad\text{and}\qquad y=2x. Compute \iint_R y\,dA.

First find the intersection points: x^2=2x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x(x-2)=0. So x=0\qquad\text{or}\qquad x=2. On 0\le x\le2, the line y=2x lies above the parabola y=x^2. Therefore R=\{(x,y):0\le x\le2,\ x^2\le y\le 2x\}. Set up the integral:
\iint_R y\,dA \amp= \int_0^2\int_{x^2}^{2x} y\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^2\left[\frac12y^2\right]_{x^2}^{2x}\,dx \amp= \frac12\int_0^2(4x^2-x^4)\,dx \amp= \frac12\left[\frac43x^3-\frac15x^5\right]_0^2 \amp= \frac12\left(\frac{32}{3}-\frac{32}{5}\right) \amp= \boxed{\frac{32}{15}}.

The same region can be described the other way. Since y=2x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x=\frac y2, and y=x^2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x=\sqrt y on the right-hand branch, the reversed description is R=\left\{(x,y):0\le y\le4,\ \frac y2\le x\le \sqrt y\right\}. So the same integral is \boxed{\iint_R y\,dA=\int_0^4\int_{y/2}^{\sqrt y}y\,dx\,dy.} This is the same quantity with a different slicing direction.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a circular sprinkler pattern
subsection.p: A sprinkler waters a circular garden of radius 2 meters. The water density at a point depends only on distance from the center: w(r)=10-r,\qquad 0\le r\le2. Find the total water and the average water density over the garden.
The value \frac{26}{3} is less than 10, because most of the garden is not near the center. In polar coordinates, the factor r reminds us that larger rings contain more area.

statement.p: What does dA mean in a double integral?
Why is \iint_R 1\,dA the area of R?
What does the inner integral mean geometrically in an iterated integral?
Why does changing the order of integration require redrawing the region?
What is a Type I region? What is a Type II region?
Why does polar area have the factor r in dA=r\,dr\,d\theta?
When is polar integration likely to simplify a problem?
How are mass and density related by a double integral?
Why are the moments M_y=\iint_R x\rho\,dA,\qquad M_x=\iint_R y\rho\,dA named with the opposite coordinate?
What does the average value formula say in words?
How can a function be unbounded at one point but still have a finite double integral?
Why can a function that tends to 0 at infinity still have a divergent integral?
What does the comparison test require?
Compute \int_0^3\int_0^2 (x+2y)\,dy\,dx.
Compute \int_0^1\int_0^x (x^2+y)\,dy\,dx.
Let R be the triangle with vertices (0,0),\qquad (4,0),\qquad (4,2). Set up \iint_R f(x,y)\,dA as an iterated integral in both orders.
Let R be bounded by y=x^2\qquad\text{and}\qquad y=4. Set up and evaluate \iint_R x^2\,dA.
Reverse the order of integration: \int_0^1\int_y^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
Reverse the order of integration: \int_0^4\int_{\sqrt y}^{2} f(x,y)\,dx\,dy.
Find the area of the region inside the circle x^2+y^2=9 and above the line y=0 using polar coordinates.
Evaluate \iint_D (x^2+y^2)\,dA, where D is the disk x^2+y^2\le 4.
Evaluate \iint_A \frac{1}{1+x^2+y^2}\,dA, where A is the annulus 1\le x^2+y^2\le 9.
A lamina occupies the rectangle 0\le x\le2,\qquad 0\le y\le3, with density \rho(x,y)=1+x. Find its mass.
For the lamina in Problem 10, find M_x,\qquad M_y, and its center of mass.
A probability density on the square 0\le x\le1,\qquad 0\le y\le1 is p(x,y)=C(x+y). Find C, and then find the probability that x+y\le1.
Find the average value of f(x,y)=x+y over the triangle with vertices (0,0),\qquad (1,0),\qquad (0,1).
Decide whether the improper integral converges: \iint_{\mathbb R^2}\frac{1}{(1+x^2+y^2)^2}\,dA.
Decide whether the improper integral converges: \iint_{\mathbb R^2}\frac{1}{1+x^2+y^2}\,dA.
Decide whether the improper integral over the unit disk converges: \iint_{x^2+y^2\le1}\frac{1}{(x^2+y^2)^{3/4}}\,dA.
Decide whether the improper integral over the unit disk converges: \iint_{x^2+y^2\le1}\frac{1}{x^2+y^2}\,dA.
Use the Gaussian integral result to evaluate \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-4x^2}\,dx.

subsection.title: Project: estimating rainfall from gridded data
subsection.p: A storm passes over a rectangular watershed. The watershed is 0\le x\le8,\qquad 0\le y\le6, where x and y are measured in kilometers.

The region is divided into rectangles of size 2\text{ km}\times 2\text{ km}. Rainfall is measured at the center of each rectangle, in centimeters.
\begin{array}{c|cccc} \amp x=1 \amp x=3 \amp x=5 \amp x=7 \\ \hline y=5 \amp 3.2 \amp 4.1 \amp 4.6 \amp 3.8\\ y=3 \amp 2.4 \amp 3.5 \amp 5.0 \amp 4.2\\ y=1 \amp 1.1 \amp 2.0 \amp 3.1 \amp 2.7 \end{array}
Each grid cell has area 4\text{ km}^2.

subsubsection.title: Part 1. Estimate the total rainfall integral
Part 2. Convert to water volume
subsubsection.p: One centimeter of rain over one square kilometer equals 10{,}000\text{ m}^3 of water. Therefore the estimated water volume is \boxed{158.8(10{,}000)=1{,}588{,}000\text{ m}^3.}

subsubsection.title: Part 3. Estimate average rainfall
subsubsection.p: The watershed area is 8\cdot6=48\text{ km}^2. The average rainfall is \boxed{r_{\mathrm{avg}}\approx \frac{158.8}{48}\approx 3.31\text{ cm}.}

subsubsection.title: Part 4. What to hand in
subsubsection.p: Write a short report with the following pieces.

li.p: A sketch of the watershed grid.
The conversion from \text{cm}\cdot\text{km}^2 to cubic meters.
A contour-style sketch showing where the rainfall appears largest.
A paragraph explaining one possible source of error in the estimate. For example, what if the heaviest rain fell between two sensors?

conclusion.p: A double integral sees a thin region: a floor, a plate, a map, a lamina. But many objects are not thin. Air fills a room. Charge fills a solid ball. Density can vary from one shelf of a tank to another. The same adding idea is waiting there too, but the tiny pieces are no longer small rectangles. They are small boxes, and the bounds must describe a three-dimensional body. One more coordinate turns the double integral into a triple integral.
